planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained soil under dry shade; this Algerian species is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established and thrives beneath the root canopy of large trees where few other groundcovers survive.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry soil in full to partial shade, where its glossy, bronze-tinted heart-shaped leaves form a weed-suppressing mat through vigorous rhizomatous spread. Bright yellow flowers emerge in early spring before the foliage hardens, offering one of the earliest color displays in the shaded garden. Unlike most epimediums, E. perralderianum is semi-evergreen and retains attractive foliage well into winter, especially in milder parts of its hardiness range. Cut back old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to showcase the spring flower display; once established, this species is among the most drought- and root-competition-tolerant of all groundcovers.
sources: American Horticultural Society A-Z Encyclopedia of Garden Plants

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; red barrenwort establishes readily and spreads steadily to form dense colonies, and is one of the most cold-hardy epimedium hybrids available.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; this popular hybrid between E. grandiflorum and E. alpinum produces small crimson-red and white bicolored flowers in spring just as striking red-tinted new foliage unfurls. The seasonal foliar interest extends into autumn when leaves again take on red and bronze tints before going fully dormant in colder zones. Cut back old leaves in late winter before new growth emerges to give the delicate spring flowers full prominence. Red barrenwort is one of the most cold-hardy epimedium hybrids, making it a reliable choice for gardens in zones 4–8 where other species may struggle.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in spring in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; the cultivar 'Sulphureum' with pale sulfur-yellow flowers is commonly available and is an excellent choice for brightening dark corners.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade, ideally beneath the high canopy of deciduous trees where dappled light reaches the ground; this hybrid between E. grandiflorum and E. pinnatum offers copper-yellow flowers in spring alongside striking copper-red new foliage that matures to soft green, then turns bronze-red again in autumn. The cultivar 'Sulphureum' bears pale sulfur-yellow flowers and is among the most widely grown epimediums in cultivation. Cut back old foliage in late winter to reveal the spring flower display. Once established, versicolor barrenwort tolerates significant summer dryness and root competition, making it a reliable choice for difficult dry-shade situations.
sources: American Horticultural Society A-Z Encyclopedia of Garden Plants

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist to moderately dry, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; partridgeberry requires acid conditions (pH 4.5–5.5) and thrives in native woodland gardens, beneath conifers, or alongside mosses and ferns.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this native North American trailing evergreen forms a low, dense mat studded with paired white funnel-shaped flowers in late spring that give rise to brilliant scarlet twin berries in fall and winter — some of the most ornamental fruit of any native groundcover. The fused double berries, each bearing two calyxes, are the defining characteristic of the genus and are edible but insipid. Partridgeberry is beloved by wildlife, particularly ruffed grouse and wild turkeys. It spreads slowly by rooting stems and is best grown in native woodland gardens, rock gardens in shade, or alongside mosses and ferns in acid woodland conditions.

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions in spring in cool, moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in full to partial shade; twinflower demands consistently cool root temperatures and high humidity, and succeeds best in northern gardens and Pacific Northwest conditions — it will not tolerate summer heat or drought.

directions: Plant in cool, moist, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; twinflower is a delicate trailing boreal woodland plant that spreads by slender rooting stems to form a fine-textured mat of small rounded leaves. In summer, pairs of tiny pink pendant bell-shaped flowers arise on forked stems above the foliage — sweetly fragrant, exquisite in detail, and perfectly matched to their Y-shaped stalks. Named for Carl Linnaeus himself, who considered it his favorite plant, L. borealis is a rewarding challenge best suited to cool northern and Pacific Northwest gardens. It will not tolerate summer heat, drought, or compacted or alkaline soils; mulch heavily with pine needles or leaf mold to maintain the cool, moist root zone it requires.
